case_name: State of New Hampshire v. John Doyle
transcript_text:
This is case 2024-0518, state of New Hampshire versus John Doyle.

Good afternoon.

Good afternoon, Mayor. Please, the court.

My name is Thomas Barnard. I represent John Doyle.

I've reserved two minutes for rebuttal.

Part 1 Article 2B heightened the standard that a litigant must meet in order to pierce the privileges.

As this court held in Zarella, that constitutional provision favors non-production and non-disclosure.

Here, the state had to meet the essential needs standard, which has two components.

A compelling justification and a showing that the targeted information is not available from another source.

I plan to make three points this afternoon.

First, there was no compelling justification to pierce the privileges.

Second, the targeted information was available from another source.

And third, even if there was an essential need, the trial court was required to conduct in Canberra review.

Even before Part 1 Article 2B was enacted, this court's cases established that a litigant must present a case-specific justification to pierce the privilege.

Here, the state advanced no case-specific justification.

Should the trial court have addressed the waiver issue first?

Not necessarily. I mean, I think it was within the trial court's discretion to address the essential need argument and having addressed that, not to address the waiver.

It's unfortunate. It may result in more delay.

Well, can we decide that issue as a manner of law?

The waiver argument? Yes.

Yeah. I don't think there is much merit at all for the waiver argument.

A defendant does not waive the privileges merely because his lawyer raises competence as an issue.

Lawyers ethically require to raise competence as an issue for one.

And the second point I would make is that when a lawyer raises competence, the lawyer isn't making a claim that the client isn't confident.

They're simply saying, I have a bona fide doubt this is an issue.

The lawyer might raise competence and then argue that the client is confident.

I've had several clients who I have raised the claim but then argue that the client is confident because that's the client's objective.

Here, Lampinianio testified that he wanted Doyle's records merely because he wants the records of everyone he evaluates.

There was no case-specific justification.

And there could not be here.

Motion for non-emergency civil commitment must allege two things.

First, a current mental illness.

And second, a recent dangerous act.

When the trial court here granted the state's motion for Doyle's records, those records were over five years old.

And the most recent allegation of any dangerous act was also more than five years old.

So if the state was in a position where it could not, regardless of the content of the records,

file a petition, a successful petition for non-emergency civil commitment,

then by definition it could have no essential need for Doyle's records.

The targeted information was not available from another source.

The state already had reports from three more recent psychiatric evaluations,

two of which...

Can you address the preservation issue?

Sure. On the unavailability from another source, sure.

So the preservation requirement applies to issues.

It does not apply to specific arguments.

If it were otherwise, then a litigant on appeal would be limited to just parroting the arguments that were made below.

And that wouldn't be helpful to this court, and it wouldn't be helpful to resolve the issues.

The issue of whether the state satisfied the essential need test was clearly preserved.

Two of the psychological evaluations summarized the relevant portions of the records at issue that the state sought.

But Lampiniano admitted that he failed to conduct a thorough review of those evaluations.

Now the state argues that in general, primary records are preferable to summaries,

but as this court has held, invasion of the privilege can never be justified

just because a patient's medical records might be the best evidence of the targeted information.

Finally, even if there was an essential need, the trial court was required to conduct in-camera review.

Under Zarella, the disclosure of records to a litigant is a distinct issue

from the production of those records to the court for in-camera review.

And this court has repeatedly held that trial courts must conduct in-camera review

before disclosing confidential records.

And the reason is obvious, as this court recognized in Zarella,

much of what a physician learns from his patient may be both embarrassing

and of little real consequence to society.

Doyle's records here from 2016 to 2019 surely contained at least some information

that was completely irrelevant to the question of whether in 2024

he suffered from a current mental illness or had committed a recent dangerous act.

I have exhausted my comments.

Unless the court has questions, I would wave the balance.

Well, do you have a plain error argument if we conclude that the second part

of the analysis is not preserved?

Yes. I mean, for the reasons I've said, I do think it's preserved.

If the court concludes that that component of the issue is not preserved,

I do think it satisfies the plain error standards.

These records, these evaluations from the OFE and from Doyle's retained expert

were part of the record.

The court was aware of them.

They were the whole basis for the court's findings of the incompetence

and lack of being restored and dangerous.

So the court should have been well aware that the records at issue

were thoroughly and completely summarized within those evaluations.

The state suggests, as I understand it, that the applicable standard of review

is unsustainable exercise of discretion.

This is basically the admission of evidence.

I think the standard of review is mixed.

In terms of factual findings, certainly the standard of review

is an unsustainable exercise of discretion.

A big part of the basis of Doyle's claim is Part 1 Article 2B

of the New Hampshire constitutional issues are reviewed de novo.

So to the extent that the trial court's ruling here reflects

an interpretation of Part 1 Article 2B, to the extent it reflects

interpretations of law on the meaning of the essential need test,

that review is de novo.

And to the extent that the trial court's findings

regarding a compelling justification or unavailability from another source

reflect factual findings, those factual findings are not sustainable

even under a deferential standard.

What's your perspective on the fact in terms of the in-camera review?

The fact that the records went to the expert, not the parties,

and or that the trial court essentially said,

I don't really know what I'm looking at or words to that effect.

Yeah, yeah.

So the fact that it went to the expert and not the parties,

I don't think changes the analysis.

The expert here was selected by the state and was,

for all intents and purposes, a state agent.

A disclosure of an alleged victim's privileged records to the defense attorney,

that implicates that person's constitutional rights to privacy

and their privileges even if there's a court order

that would only be disclosed to the attorney,

the agent of the party, and not the party him or herself.

The second part of your question concerned the technical nature of the records.

Privilege records are almost always technical,

but a trial court's obligation to conduct in-camera review

exists regardless of the technical nature of the records.

McDonald involved over 2,000 pages of medical and mental health records.

CT involved toxicology and medical reports.

Desclose involved all the plaintiffs' psychiatric and psychological records.

Paying involved medical records.

And the reason that the court is still competent to conduct this review

is because as this court held in refossey,

the condition of dangerousness for purposes of civil commitment

is not a medical concept but rather a legal one.

Which are going to be different if Mr. Dull had retained an expert

who then reviewed the records in order to form an opinion as to

his position with respect to dangerousness,

with the state being entitled to that information,

is that what the case?

And then presented that expert's testimony in court?

Yes.

Then in that circumstance, I think the waiver argument would be

much stronger than it is here.

Unless the court has further questions.

Thank you.

Thank you for your minutes.

Good afternoon, and may it please the court,

Adriana McCullough for the State of New Hampshire.

I'd like to start by pointing out to this court

that the state does not disagree that Part 1 Article 2B

applies to the defendant's medical records.

But this court held in Zarella,

and in the other cases about essential need,

that when the state wants to pierce a defendant's or a

witness's or a victim's physician-patient privilege

to review medical records,

that the test this court uses to determine if that privilege

can be pierced is the essential need test.

And that's exactly what happened here when this court

remanded this case for review by the trial court.

The trial court held a hearing at which the state presented

evidence, establishing that there was an essential need

for that particular physician, Dr. Lampagnano,

to review the defendant's medical and mental health records,

that the OFE had and that Dr. Goudry had to determine

if that physician could certify him to begin the probate

process of being involuntarily committed for treatment

based on his dangerousness finding,

that the trial court had made prior to this hearing.

Isn't Judge St. Hillar's order really a per se

order and under these circumstances,

these records are always going to be an essential need

for the state whenever they seek to civilly

commit somebody on that basis?

It is not because the trial court had to make

specific factual findings to issue that order.

The state did not ask the trial court at that hearing

to hold that in every case going forward,

these sorts of evaluations require the trial court

to provide the records to the state,

and the state is not asking this court to hold that

on appeal.

But isn't that sort of embedded in what was presented

to the trial court that this is,

it's essentially the standard of care

requires a full record review.

So isn't Judge St. Hillar's question

that always going to apply?

I can't say, and that's because it will depend

on whatever physician is testifying

or whatever evidence is presented at that hearing.

Does the standard of care deviate?

Typically no, although I think in recent news

we see that the standard of, for example,

vaccines can change based on who is in the federal government

and so I could, it is possible that the standard of care

for psychiatric and law evaluations

could change in the future.

It certainly hasn't changed as far as I know,

and I would imagine that any evaluator would testify

at a hearing that the best source of information

about the defendant's medical and mental health

would be from his medical records.

However, there...

So it is per se.

But no one is asking this court to hold that.

And if that, I mean, I guess on some level, yes.

Would it be elevating form over substance

to have an evidentiary hearing every single time

the state needs medical records

to do one of these evaluations?

Perhaps.

However, as this court has acknowledged in Zarela

and as the defendant certainly argues here,

these records are protected under the Constitution

and they're protected by two separate statutes.

And so I don't know that it's elevating form over substance

if this sort of hearing ensures

that the court and the state and the defense attorney,

if he doesn't have the records either,

are not piercing a defendant's privilege unnecessarily.

And so this is that safeguard.

You don't need to...

For the state to be successful in this appeal,

this court does not need to hold

that in every hearing like this going forward,

the records must be disclosed.

I see why it feels that way,

but you don't have to decide that.

In this case, there was evidence presented

that there was an essential need for these records

based on evidence about there being

a compelling justification from these records.

As you pointed out, Justice MacDonald,

it is about this being the adequate standard of care

to perform one of these evaluations.

But the other piece of that that Dr. Lampagnano talked about

was that psychiatric conditions can wax and wane over time.

And that is something that the records

would be the best source of information for.

When compared at least minimally

to the defendant's self-report,

especially in these evaluations.

I don't know...

I know that we've provided in our appendices

the various evaluations that the defendant went through,

but he does provide some self-reporting

about his own mental health experiences.

And some of those are corroborated by the medical records

and some of that information is not.

And honestly, in Dr. Gujri's summary,

which I know I may be getting into my second argument too early,

but in Dr. Gujri's summary of these records,

she doesn't make clear what is a self-report from the defendant

versus what is present in the medical records

versus what is something she observed during the evaluation.

And so having Dr. Lampagnano have the ability

to review these other psychiatric records

could tell him what is something the defendant

self-reported in these evaluations

or in his own evaluation with the defendant

and what is or is not supported

in that self-evaluation by the records.

But he acknowledged that he hadn't read those competency reports.

So isn't that speculative?

He hadn't read them at the time of the hearing.

Presumably he would have read them

if that was all he had to do this evaluation.

And the state had made it so that he could have those evaluations

to conduct his evaluation.

But I think, based on his testimony,

he would have preferred to have the records

at the same time that he was reviewing those evaluations

to do what I've just described,

which is to compare the self-reports

that the defendant gives to him

and to these other evaluators alongside the medical records

to see what the defendant's own interpretation

of his mental health and dangerousness is

in comparison to what other professionals have seen

in these admittedly short bursts of time

that they've been able to evaluate him or observe him.

Do we know what Dr. Guidry reviewed

when assessing the defendant's storability?

I know some of them off the top of my head.

They are listed in her report very specifically,

but she had records that the OFE did not even had.

She had the River Bend records from the restoration period,

which the OFE had,

and she also had records from his time at Concord Hospital

where he was, I think he was held there

for about 10 days while they were trying to get him

into a bed at New Hampshire Hospital

in their psychiatric unit.

I'm not sure because I haven't seen these records,

but I think it's a 10-day span of time.

She had those. The OFE did not have those when it did.

I think it's a 10-day span of time

that she was able to evaluate him

and observe him and observe him.

I think it's a 10-day span of time

that she was able to evaluate him

based on its summary of the records

and what it listed it reviewed.

She already had additional records

that Dr. Lampignano wanted,

because from his perspective,

that would have also given a very clear picture

from 2019 into 2020

of the defendant's mental health

closest in time to when he was doing his evaluation.

Dr. Barnard, well, to answer your question first,

the state's position would be

that the defendant has waived the privilege

to all of these records.

However, if you or if this court would like to

determine the outcome of this case

based on that argument,

the state would request an opportunity

to brief that issue.

The state did not brief that issue in this particular brief

based on the arguments that were made

in the defendant's brief

and based on the order that the trial court wrote.

So do you think we can review that

or do we need to send it back to the superior court?

Frankly, I do think you would need

to send it back to the superior court

because your job as the appellate court

would be to review his order on that particular issue

based on the facts that were developed at that hearing.

So I would imagine

part of the state's brief would say

you need to remand it first to have him

or have the judge in that court

issue an order on that particular argument

before this court can decide,

as a matter of law or as a matter of fact

that this defendant has waived

to these records by providing it to these other people.

But certainly the state made

that argument at the trial court down below.

And I know that this court instructed

the trial court to find both that

and essential need when it remanded it the first time.

Anyway, in terms

of the unavailable from another source

the state does believe that this should be

reviewed under plain error because it is not

preserved for review. There is a distinction

between preserving issues

for appeal and arguments for appeal.

Well, the trial court addressed availability

of the records, did he not?

He did, but not based on this specific argument

that there were summaries

of the records provided

in these reports that Dr. Lampagnano

had had.

I know that Dr. Lampagnano testified

he had not thoroughly reviewed those reports

but I don't believe anyone asked him

if he had reviewed minimally the summaries

or if he knew what records were even

potentially available to him from

or about the defendant. And neither party

argued to the trial court about whether

these summaries were or were not

an alternative source of this information.

And so for this court to find that

at the first instance based on

no facts presented about that at the hearing

and no arguments made about that at the hearing

would not be appropriate in this context

given that you are reviewing

for an unsustainable exercise of discretion

in the trial court's order.

So

if this court does review that under plain error

this error was not plain

because I already sort of

talked about this so I apologize for being out of order

but these summaries

are not an adequate or an alternative

source of this information.

As I explained the OFE did not

even have any records for the defendant

when it conducted its competency

evaluation in the beginning of this whole process.

And

that evaluator wrote many times in her report

that if I received those reports

or those records my outcome

or my opinion could change.

And she based basically her entire

findings on his self-reporting

and how he presented in that evaluation.

By the time we were at the restoration

evaluation she did have records from

Riverbend but they were only from that

years long period

of his restoration so none of the records

before his competency was raised.

Dr. Gujri's

summary appears to talk about his records

from 2016 through 2019

and then his Riverbend records

during the restoration period

in these 10 days of records from Concord Hospital.

So her summary is

more robust because she had more information

but as I've already sort of pointed out

she draws medical conclusions

about the defendant that it's unclear

what the source of that is from.

It's unclear if she's referring to records

where other professionals saw that information

or if she's taking that from his self-report

and some of those conclusions

such as him she finds at one point

that he says

jail is a deterrent to me so I won't be violent.

It's unclear if he just said that to her

in the evaluation if that's something

that a medical professional at one of these other places

found to be true or

if he said that to somebody else versus Dr.

Gujri.

Can you address the in-camera review issue?

Yes. So in this particular

case there are two reasons why

the trial court did not need to do an in-camera

review in this context.

One of them is that this court has held

that the purpose of an in-camera review

is to ensure that irrelevant

or non-responsive records are not disclosed

to the parties.

And while it is true in this case that Dr.

Lampignano was the state's

expert, this trial court

had also ordered that the state

was not permitted to see any of the

defendant's medical records throughout this entire process.

So the state never saw the records

that Dr. Gujri or the OFE

used to conduct

their evaluations and examinations

and the court had prevented the state from getting

these records when it also prevented

Dr. Lampignano from having them initially

in the beginning before we got all the way

where we are now. And even in this order

that the trial court issued the state would never

have seen those medical records without filing

its own motion, arguing there was

an essential need for it to see the records.

So in that context

these records were not going to the parties.

They were going to the OFE.

I mean to Dr. Lampignano.

And the second point is that

these records were being used

by Dr. Lampignano to make a medical finding.

Granted some of those

findings are rooted in case law that

explain what dangerousness is

and what the 135C process is

but Dr. Lampignano is using them

to see if the defendant had

a mental health condition

actively and if he was dangerous.

Those are medical findings based on

how the defendant has presented

in front of that evaluator

and over a course of time.

So these for the trial court

to conduct an in-camera review of records

for a legal purpose.

When trial courts are trained

in conducting in-camera reviews

to determine what is necessary for a legal purpose

such as proving serious bodily injury

or proving some other

fact of a crime. I think one of these cases

that the defense cited

on the records were being used to establish

legally that the victim was disabled

under the AFSA statute.

That is much different.

Those legal conclusions or the facts

that the state needs to prove a legal

is different than what Dr. Lampignano

is using these records for

which is to determine a mental health diagnosis

and dangerousness

and so to expect the trial court to go

through thousands of pages of records

without really knowing what it's looking for

would I think elevate

form over substance of conducting

an in-camera review in every single one of these

or would require the trial court

to in some way consult with the evaluator

to ask the evaluator what do you need

from these records to conduct

Imagine

some sort of a

medical issue

that doesn't implicate

the individuals

the legal issue

wouldn't there be

a basis to exclude

those components of the record?

It's possible

and the defendant could certainly make that pitch

to the OFE have irrelevant information

and frankly I would say

but he didn't have the records his counsel didn't have the records

that part of the defendant's diagnosis

in his records are redacted

and so I'm not I don't know if the hospital

but somebody had redacted the records that went to the OFE

question the trial court

have some obligation as gatekeeper

in terms of

or private information that is not

for example this defendant has

over that issue

are provided to

here in this case

that's what the state was seeking to provide

thank you

Thank you

of 2020

segments.text: This is case 2024-0518, state of New Hampshire versus John Doyle.
Good afternoon.
Good afternoon, Mayor. Please, the court.
My name is Thomas Barnard. I represent John Doyle.
I've reserved two minutes for rebuttal.
Part 1 Article 2B heightened the standard that a litigant must meet in order to pierce the privileges.
As this court held in Zarella, that constitutional provision favors non-production and non-disclosure.
Here, the state had to meet the essential needs standard, which has two components.
A compelling justification and a showing that the targeted information is not available from another source.
I plan to make three points this afternoon.
First, there was no compelling justification to pierce the privileges.
Second, the targeted information was available from another source.
And third, even if there was an essential need, the trial court was required to conduct in Canberra review.
Even before Part 1 Article 2B was enacted, this court's cases established that a litigant must present a case-specific justification to pierce the privilege.
Here, the state advanced no case-specific justification.
Should the trial court have addressed the waiver issue first?
Not necessarily. I mean, I think it was within the trial court's discretion to address the essential need argument and having addressed that, not to address the waiver.
It's unfortunate. It may result in more delay.
Well, can we decide that issue as a manner of law?
The waiver argument? Yes.
Yeah. I don't think there is much merit at all for the waiver argument.
A defendant does not waive the privileges merely because his lawyer raises competence as an issue.
Lawyers ethically require to raise competence as an issue for one.
And the second point I would make is that when a lawyer raises competence, the lawyer isn't making a claim that the client isn't confident.
They're simply saying, I have a bona fide doubt this is an issue.
The lawyer might raise competence and then argue that the client is confident.
I've had several clients who I have raised the claim but then argue that the client is confident because that's the client's objective.
Here, Lampinianio testified that he wanted Doyle's records merely because he wants the records of everyone he evaluates.
There was no case-specific justification.
And there could not be here.
Motion for non-emergency civil commitment must allege two things.
First, a current mental illness.
And second, a recent dangerous act.
When the trial court here granted the state's motion for Doyle's records, those records were over five years old.
And the most recent allegation of any dangerous act was also more than five years old.
So if the state was in a position where it could not, regardless of the content of the records,
file a petition, a successful petition for non-emergency civil commitment,
then by definition it could have no essential need for Doyle's records.
The targeted information was not available from another source.
The state already had reports from three more recent psychiatric evaluations,
two of which...
Can you address the preservation issue?
Sure. On the unavailability from another source, sure.
So the preservation requirement applies to issues.
It does not apply to specific arguments.
If it were otherwise, then a litigant on appeal would be limited to just parroting the arguments that were made below.
And that wouldn't be helpful to this court, and it wouldn't be helpful to resolve the issues.
The issue of whether the state satisfied the essential need test was clearly preserved.
Two of the psychological evaluations summarized the relevant portions of the records at issue that the state sought.
But Lampiniano admitted that he failed to conduct a thorough review of those evaluations.
Now the state argues that in general, primary records are preferable to summaries,
but as this court has held, invasion of the privilege can never be justified
just because a patient's medical records might be the best evidence of the targeted information.
Finally, even if there was an essential need, the trial court was required to conduct in-camera review.
Under Zarella, the disclosure of records to a litigant is a distinct issue
from the production of those records to the court for in-camera review.
And this court has repeatedly held that trial courts must conduct in-camera review
before disclosing confidential records.
And the reason is obvious, as this court recognized in Zarella,
much of what a physician learns from his patient may be both embarrassing
and of little real consequence to society.
Doyle's records here from 2016 to 2019 surely contained at least some information
that was completely irrelevant to the question of whether in 2024
he suffered from a current mental illness or had committed a recent dangerous act.
I have exhausted my comments.
Unless the court has questions, I would wave the balance.
Well, do you have a plain error argument if we conclude that the second part
of the analysis is not preserved?
Yes. I mean, for the reasons I've said, I do think it's preserved.
If the court concludes that that component of the issue is not preserved,
I do think it satisfies the plain error standards.
These records, these evaluations from the OFE and from Doyle's retained expert
were part of the record.
The court was aware of them.
They were the whole basis for the court's findings of the incompetence
and lack of being restored and dangerous.
So the court should have been well aware that the records at issue
were thoroughly and completely summarized within those evaluations.
The state suggests, as I understand it, that the applicable standard of review
is unsustainable exercise of discretion.
This is basically the admission of evidence.
I think the standard of review is mixed.
In terms of factual findings, certainly the standard of review
is an unsustainable exercise of discretion.
A big part of the basis of Doyle's claim is Part 1 Article 2B
of the New Hampshire constitutional issues are reviewed de novo.
So to the extent that the trial court's ruling here reflects
an interpretation of Part 1 Article 2B, to the extent it reflects
interpretations of law on the meaning of the essential need test,
that review is de novo.
And to the extent that the trial court's findings
regarding a compelling justification or unavailability from another source
reflect factual findings, those factual findings are not sustainable
even under a deferential standard.
What's your perspective on the fact in terms of the in-camera review?
The fact that the records went to the expert, not the parties,
and or that the trial court essentially said,
I don't really know what I'm looking at or words to that effect.
Yeah, yeah.
So the fact that it went to the expert and not the parties,
I don't think changes the analysis.
The expert here was selected by the state and was,
for all intents and purposes, a state agent.
A disclosure of an alleged victim's privileged records to the defense attorney,
that implicates that person's constitutional rights to privacy
and their privileges even if there's a court order
that would only be disclosed to the attorney,
the agent of the party, and not the party him or herself.
The second part of your question concerned the technical nature of the records.
Privilege records are almost always technical,
but a trial court's obligation to conduct in-camera review
exists regardless of the technical nature of the records.
McDonald involved over 2,000 pages of medical and mental health records.
CT involved toxicology and medical reports.
